stem: In a employee training study comparing Training Program X vs. Y: Overall, Treatment Y has a higher performance improvement (73.3% vs 64.2%). However, when stratified by initial skill level: In junior employees (n_X=20, n_Y=120): Treatment X achieves 87% vs Treatment Y's 78%. In senior employees (n_X=100, n_Y=30): Treatment X achieves 60% vs Treatment Y's 55%. The true population proportion of junior employees is uncertain, estimated between 31% and 43%. Which treatment is actually better, and by how much?

ground_truth.answer: This is Simpson's Paradox. Treatment X is better in BOTH subgroups. The aggregate reversal occurs because X is disproportionately applied to senior employees (harder cases). Adjusted difference (X - Y): [0.063, 0.067], point estimate: 0.065. Treatment X is genuinely better by 6.5 percentage points.

solution_steps: Aggregate rates: X=64.2%, Y=73.3% (Y appears better).
Stratified: In junior employees: X=87% > Y=78%. In senior employees: X=60% > Y=55%.
X is better in BOTH subgroups — this is Simpson's Paradox.
The paradox arises because Training Program X is applied more to senior employees (lower baseline rate), skewing the aggregate.
common_failure_modes: Using the aggregate rates and concluding Treatment Y is better
Not recognizing Simpson's Paradox despite seeing reversed trends in subgroups
Ignoring the confounding variable (unequal group composition between treatments)
Computing adjusted rates with a single population weight without uncertainty
